problem: Should each feature flag get its own REST endpoint?
consensus: A REST endpoint per feature reads clean and cache-friendly. Give each flag its own route, version them, done. It is the idiomatic pattern.
advisor_answer: One endpoint returns all flags for a user; the server decides which apply. One deep module instead of N shallow ones.
reframe_question: What if a single deep endpoint returned all of a user's flags, instead of N shallow routes you now have to version forever?
quote: the best modules are those whose interfaces are much simpler than their implementations
anecdote: Unix file I/O: five calls (open, read, write, lseek, close) hide enormous machinery (buffering, disk layout, permissions). A deep module.

problem: A delete throws when the row is already gone — how should I handle it?
consensus: Add proper error handling: catch the already-gone case, return a clear 404, log it for observability, and write a test.
advisor_answer: Redefine the operation so already-gone is success. Delete becomes idempotent; the error path disappears.
reframe_question: What would it take to redefine already-gone as success, so the error case cannot arise at all?
anecdote: Tcl's unset threw if the variable was gone, so everyone wrapped it in catch. The fix made it a no-op. Unix does the same: deleting an open file just succeeds.

problem: Reviewers keep asking for more comments — should I add them?
consensus: Push for self-documenting code (good names, small functions) and add comments where intent is not obvious. The reviewers usually have a point.
advisor_answer: Comment the non-obvious: intent, invariants, cross-module dependencies. Put the cross-cutting ones in one designNotes file.
reframe_question: Which of these comments repeat what the code already says, and which capture the non-obvious intent a reader cannot recover?
principle: Comments describe what is not obvious from the code
quote: comments should describe things that aren't obvious from the code
anecdote: Cross-module design decisions have no single home in any one class; encapsulation fights discoverability. Ousterhout's answer: a central designNotes file the modules point back to.

problem: It passes tests and hits the deadline — should I just ship it?
consensus: If it passes tests and hits the deadline, ship it and file a refactor ticket. Perfect is the enemy of shipped.
advisor_answer: Spend the ~10-20% now: design it twice, and pull the complexity into this module so its callers never see it.
reframe_question: What complexity are you pushing downstream to hit this deadline, and who pays the interest on it later?
anecdote: The tactical tornado ships features fast and leaves the complexity for everyone downstream to clean up, and gets promoted for velocity while others pay the interest.
